gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Steamed Rice Flour Dumpling (analogous to Ku Chai Kueh)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value for Ku Chai Kueh is estimated based on similar steamed rice flour dumplings with vegetable filling, as a direct entry is not available. The GI of 65 is assigned by analogy to comparable rice flour-based steamed dumplings in the University of Sydney GI Database.

hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should consume Ku Chai Kueh sparingly due to its moderate sodium content (281mg/100g) and consider requesting reduced salt and soy sauce in the filling.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Due to its medium glycemic index (65) and carbohydrate content (19.9g/100g), individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion sizes to one piece and pair with high-fibre vegetables or protein to moderate postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ku Chai Kueh has a relatively low saturated fat content (~1.8g/100g) and no trans fat, but the moderate sodium level (281mg/100g) warrants mindful portion control for individuals managing cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ku Chai Kueh is a traditional Singaporean steamed dumpling, widely enjoyed as a snack or light meal across ethnic communities in Singapore. It features a soft, chewy, semi-translucent skin made from rice flour and tapioca starch, encasing a savoury filling primarily composed of stir-fried Chinese chives (ku chai) and dried shrimp. The chives provide a distinct pungent, garlicky flavour, complemented by the umami of dried shrimp and typically seasoned with garlic, soy sauce, and sesame oil. The texture contrasts the smooth, yielding wrapper with the tender, aromatic filling. Nutritionally, each 100g provides 161 kcal, 19.9g carbohydrates, 8.33g fat, 1.7g protein, 2.7g dietary fibre, and 281mg sodium. The chives contribute antioxidant polyphenols and dietary fibre, while the dried shrimp adds umami and trace minerals. It is typically served with a chilli dipping sauce and is a common breakfast and tea-time item at hawker centres and traditional kueh stalls.
common_user_queries: How to make Ku Chai Kueh?
Is Ku Chai Kueh suitable for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Nutrient data is directly from HPB Singapore, an approved primary source for Singaporean dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database, clearly documented.
